title: Colostle - A Solo RPG Adventure - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xQKEy65zAso

Daniel (@dungeondive) explores Colostle by Nick Angel, a solo journaling RPG with a gorgeous, wildly inventive premise: a castle so colossal that fields, mountains and entire oceans fill its rooms, lit by ceiling-hung braziers serving as suns and stars. You play an explorer mapping these "room lands," seeking the rooftop battlements, while avoiding the Rooks — lumbering stone automatons whose cores hold weapons and magic. He's clear this sits firmly at the journaling end of the solo-RPG spectrum: the rules exist to feed prompts, and the fun is entirely in how much you enjoy writing.

Character creation is quick — draw cards for a calling, nature, class and weapon (Armed, Followed, Helmed or Mounted, each setting your exploration and combat card-draw counts). Play is dot-connecting: lay out exploration cards, read the art and the matching chart entries, and weave them into a journal chapter. He narrates a vivid sample run as "Carson of the One Wheels" in a giant treaded mono-wheel — a village atop ancient ruins, a storm, a found crossbow, a gargoyle snatching him to the rafters. Its strengths: a stunning setting, beautiful art, and disarming simplicity. A story-generator, not a crunchy game.

----

Colostle hands you evocative prompts and asks you to write the story yourself. Do journaling games scratch your solo itch, or do you need more mechanical crunch between you and the blank page?
